1. Vector Spaces

Definition 1. A field is a set satisfying:

  • Addition and multiplication are defined.
  • and are both commutative and associative. is distributive over .
  • contains a additive identity .
  • contains a multiplicative identity .
  • Each scalar in has an additive inverse in .
  • Every nonzero scalar in has a multiplicative inverse in .

Elements of a field are called scalars.

Definition 2. A vector space over a field is a set satisfying:

  • Addition and scalar multiplication are defined.
  • is commutative and associative. Scalar multiplication is associative and distributive over .
  • contains an additive identity .
  • for all where is the multiplicative identity in .
  • Every vector in has an additive inverse in . [TODO] isn’t this redundant by scalar multiplication
  • is closed under addition.
  • is closed under scalar multiplication.

Elements of a vector space are called vectors.

    • In the case of operators, this does not necessarily mean that . An easy counterexample is .

  • [3.56] A linear map is invertible if and only if it is both injective and surjective.

    • [3.69] For operators these three adjectives are equivalent to each other.
  • [3.59] Two finite-dimensional vector spaces over are isomorphic if and only if they have the same dimension.

  • [3.118] Row rank equals column rank

    • Nice proof from Artin (4.2.14). Show that you can specify a basis of and such that the matrix takes the form of an identity matrix at the left corner with zeros everywhere else.

1.2. Projectors

A projector is a square matrix satisfying idempotence: . The spectrum is a subset of .

Proposition 3 (Orthogonal projectors). Suppose . Then the following are equivalent:

  1. is an orthogonal projector
  2. and are orthogonal.

A nonorthogonal (oblique) projector has range and nullspace that are not orthogonal. For example, has range spanned by and kernel spanned by .

1.5. Duality

  • A linear functional on is an element of .

  • The dual space of , denoted as is the vector space of all linear functionals on .

    • I.e.
    • We have

  • If is a basis of , then the dual basis of is the list where each is a linear functional on given (Kronecker delta).

    • We can prove that the dual basis is a basis of .
    • I.e. all linear functionals can be expressed as a linear combination of , which is kind of trivial since any linear map is uniquely expressed by how it acts on each basis element.
  • If , then the dual map of is the linear map given by for a .

(4)

into some vector space , denoted as , such that is bilinear. Furthermore, it would be nice if each basis of when combined with each basis of produced a basis of . Specifically, we would like a basis , implying should equal .

Definition 4 (Bilinear Functional). A bilinear functional on is a function such that is bilinear.

The set of such functions forms a vector space denoted
